author: carlosala

body:
I think that what you say is not correct Marc.
```zsh
is_plugin() {
  local base_dir=$1
  local name=$2
  builtin test -f $base_dir/plugins/$name/$name.plugin.zsh \
    || builtin test -f $base_dir/plugins/$name/_$name
}
```
This will return correctly in both cases. Maybe we should modify the logic here
author: carlosala

body:
That would change how we add plugins to `$fpath`.

If we only return true if `$name.plugin.zsh` exists, plugins without these files (like most completion plugins) will not get added to `$fpath` and break.
If we only return true if `_$name` exists, plugins without `_$name` like the `genpass` plugin will not work correctly if they're not added to `$fpath`.

Also the proposed change does not decouple the path to be sourced from the check if the file exists, so it moves the logic to two places (`is_plugin` and the `source` line).

We could turn this into a `for` loop but given that we only need to check 2 locations that seems wasteful. It's better to leave it as is.
author: mcornella

body: As far as I understand, the single problem with using `is_plugin` is that we would be trying to source a file that doesn't exists, the ones that don't have `.plugin.zsh` and have `_plugin`. We can implement a new function to use the `builtin test` or just let it as it is.
author: carlosala

body: Yeah that's the problem. We could also have a function check if the plugin file exists and loads it like you say, but we'd only be moving the logic somewhere else.

body:
Are the braces really necessary? I believe all commands of the pipe are run in the background without `{}` no? For instance, this example:
```sh
sleep 10 | tee /dev/null    # waits 10 seconds
sleep 10 | tee /dev/null &  # immediately finishes
sleep 10 | tee /dev/null &| # immediately finishes without output
```
author: mcornella

body:
BTW documenting this for future eyes, [the docs are a bit confusing here](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Shell-Grammar.html#Simple-Commands-_0026-Pipelines):

> If a sublist is terminated by a ‘&’, ‘&|’, or ‘&!’, the shell executes the last pipeline in it in the background, and does not wait for it to finish (note the difference from other shells which execute the whole sublist in the background)

It is not referring to a series of commands piped together, but chained like so:
```sh
cp a b && mv b c && rm a &
```
[See this StackExchange answer](https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/88235/487474) for more.

body:
```
# parse index file(s) [https://git-scm.com/docs/index-format]
  X=$(xxd -p -l4 -s12 "$gitdir/$indexf" 2>/dev/null) # extension signature
  [[ $? -ne 0 ]] && return 2
  if [[ "$X" == "6c696e6b" ]]; then # "link" extension --> split/shared index
```
As shown in [index-format](https://git-scm.com/docs/index-format):

- After the 12-byte header is a 4-byte extension signature, hence `X=$(xxd -p -l4 -s12 ...)`;
- The [split index](https://git-scm.com/docs/index-format#_split_index) has an extension signature literally spelled "`link`" in ASCII whose HEX value is `0x6c696e6b`, hence `[[ "$X" == "6c696e6b" ]]`. We can use `xxd -r` to convert it back from HEX to ASCII, but it's obviously superfluous.

body:
Hi Ben, you were actually very close: you don't need to use glob modifiers, since these are also applicable to parameters and they don't use a subcommand:
```suggestion
  local zdot="${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}"
  local zshrc="${${:-"${zdot}/.zshrc"}:A}"
  awk "$awk_script" "$zshrc" > "${zdot}/.zshrc.new" \
  && command cp -f "$zshrc" "${zdot}/.zshrc.bck" \
  && command mv -f "${zdot}/.zshrc.new" "$zshrc"
```
which is less ugly-looking by introducing a new `local` variable.

body:
This was added recently in #12045 (very nice work getting Wayland support for bgnotify). This PR will no longer show the Alacritty icon, but the user can easily re-add this functionality by adding this in their ~/.zshrc file:

```
function bgnotify_formatted {
  ## $1=exit_status, $2=command, $3=elapsed_time
  [ $1 -eq 0 ] && title="Great success" || title="Command failed"
  bgnotify "$title - took ${3}s" "$2" Alacritty
}
plugins=(git bgnotify sudo)  # for example
```

I'm not against having a set of reasonable fall-back icons, but I don't think it's trivial:

* There are [dozens of different terminal emulators](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_terminal_emulators). How many are a reasonable number to hard-code here?
* Can we ensure icons are somewhat reliably installed in accessible places across different distributions and OSes? For instance, the icon example on the [notifu project website](https://www.paralint.com/projects/notifu/) is `%SYSTEMROOT%\system32\shell32.dll,43` - hardly Unix standard
author: sjoblomj

Regarding linux: `notify-send` can take a file path or an app name as icon, and we can expect those icons to be in `/usr/share/icons` or `/usr/share/pixmaps`. The issue I see is that there's no reliable way (that I know of) of getting the appropriate icon name for each terminal, and we'd have to maintain a mapping of env vars -> icon names.

Another approach that could work, is to add an icon for bgnotify in this repo, and have that be the default if `icon` is not explicitly set by the user.

body:
This regex here fails when the version numbers begin with `0`, in which case the result are 0s:
```py
>>> coerce("v2.03.4")
Version(major=2, minor=0, patch=0, prerelease=None, build=None)
```

The `0|[1-9]` part should go inside an additional parenthesis:
```suggestion
BASEVERSION = re.compile(
    r"""[vV]?
    (?P<major>(0|[1-9])\d*)
    (\.
    (?P<minor>(0|[1-9])\d*)
    (\.
    (?P<patch>(0|[1-9])\d*)
    )?
    )?
    """,
    re.VERBOSE,
)
```

With this change the `coerce` function works again:
```py
>>> coerce("v2.3.4")
Version(major=2, minor=3, patch=4, prerelease=None, build=None)
>>> coerce("v2.03.4")
Version(major=2, minor=3, patch=4, prerelease=None, build=None)
>>> coerce("zsh2.03.4")
Version(major=2, minor=3, patch=4, prerelease=None, build=None)
>>> coerce("zsh-2.03.4")

    raise ValueError(f"{version} is not valid SemVer string")
ValueError: v2.3.4 is not valid SemVer string
```

body: On Apple Silicon macs `brew` is now in `/opt/homebrew/bin` instead of `/usr/local/bin` and it's not in the `PATH` by default, so this command fails and `command not found: chruby` is shown, while `chruby` could be loaded from the [new default Homebrew prefix](https://docs.brew.sh/Installation). And it makes more sense to check to see if the `brew` command is available only before shelling out to the command, so I've moved it below.
author: dskecse

body:
This will continue to work on older Intel-based macs, as this Homebrew prefix is covered by checking the `/usr/local/share/chruby/chruby.sh` path [below](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/chruby/chruby.plugin.zsh#L49), and the following symlinks exist on those macs (I've double-checked it on mine):

```
/usr/local/share/chruby -> /usr/local/Cellar/chruby/0.3.9/share/chruby
/usr/local/opt/chruby -> /usr/local/Cellar/chruby/0.3.9
```

body:
```zsh
if type compdef &>/dev/null; then
    _pnpm_completion () {
        local reply
        local si=$IFS

        IFS=$'\n' reply=($(COMP_CWORD="$((CURRENT-1))" COMP_LINE="$BUFFER" COMP_POINT="$CURSOR" SHELL=zsh pnpm completion-server -- "${words[@]}"))
        IFS=$si

        if [ "$reply" = "__tabtab_complete_files__" ]; then
            _files
        else
            _describe 'values' reply
        fi
    }
    # When called by the Zsh completion system, this will end with
    # "loadautofunc" when initially autoloaded and "shfunc" later on, otherwise,
    # the script was "eval"-ed so use "compdef" to register it with the
    # completion system
    if [[ $zsh_eval_context == *func ]]; then
        _pnpm_completion "$@"
    else
        compdef _pnpm_completion pnpm
    fi
fi
```
This is the one from the official doc

body:
## Pull request overview This PR aims to prevent the creation of pull requests when there are no relevant file changes after applying upstream dependency updates. It refactors the update workflow by extracting a `repo_is_clean()` helper method and adding a conditional check to only proceed with YAML updates and PR creation when actual changes are detected. **Key changes:** - Extracted `repo_is_clean()` method from `add_and_commit()` for reusability - Added conditional logic to check if repository has changes before updating YAML and creating PR - Updated quote style from single to double quotes for dictionary access (consistency improvement) --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

You can also share your feedback on Copilot code review for a chance to win a $100 gift card. [Take the survey](https://survey.alchemer.com/s3/8579117/Copilot-Code-Review-In-product-survey-Nov-2025).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The `repo_is_clean()` check is performed immediately after `__apply_upstream_changes()`, but the YAML file update happens inside the conditional block. This creates a logic error: if upstream changes result in modifications, the YAML file won't be updated to reflect the new version.

The correct flow should be:
1. Apply upstream changes
2. Update YAML file with new version
3. Check if repo is clean (to handle cases where upstream changes had no effect)
4. If not clean, commit and create PR

Move the `__update_yaml()` call before the `repo_is_clean()` check on line 225.
```suggestion
                    # Update dependencies.yml file
                    self.__update_yaml(
                        f"tag:{new_version}" if is_tag else status["version"]
                    )

                    if not Git.repo_is_clean():
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The `repo_is_clean()` method catches all `CommandRunner.Exception` exceptions and returns `False`, assuming any error means the repo is dirty. However, if the git command fails for a different reason (e.g., not in a git repository, permission issues), this will incorrectly report the repo as dirty rather than propagating the actual error.

Consider checking the specific exit code or error message to distinguish between "repo has changes" (exit code 1) vs other errors, or document this behavior explicitly in the docstring.

While looking into this, I noticed that gitignore.io is a CDN layer that will then redirect to the appropriate URL. Given that, I think we should opt to just remove the toptal-specific URL and let the CDN do it's thing.

```sh
 curl -I https://www.gitignore.io/api
HTTP/2 301
date: Sun, 18 Jan 2026 19:41:25 GMT
location: https://www.toptal.com/developers/gitignore/api
report-to: {"group":"cf-nel","max_age":604800,"endpoints":[{"url":"https://a.nel.cloudflare.com/report/v4?s=a3pSPSo8bnUAyf6nLymywxhHLbwTYPJEKaydloZf1aWl4ewUL4i80BDWHMGvSTiWgMsGK2o0ihrIJHhKNsxpEOpwvuvc2oEQMOo3PZrDgUg%3D"}]}
nel: {"report_to":"cf-nel","success_fraction":0.0,"max_age":604800}
server: cloudflare
cf-ray: 9c0091d93c8be2d7-SEA
alt-svc: h3=":443"; ma=86400

```

This should simplify things so that we don't need to worry about needing to think about a fallback because the CDN should handle it. Would you be up for updating the PR to wap the endpoint URI? I'd like you to get credit for the change. :-)

body: ## Pull request overview This PR adds GitHub API authentication using a GH_TOKEN environment variable to avoid rate limiting issues when making requests to the GitHub API. The token is provided via GitHub App authentication configured in the workflow. ### Key Changes - Introduced GH_TOKEN environment variable and HEADERS constant with authentication - Updated two GitHub API request calls to include authentication headers - Minor whitespace cleanup (removed trailing whitespace) --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The Authorization header should use "token" instead of "Bearer" for GitHub API authentication. GitHub's REST API expects personal access tokens and GitHub App tokens to use the "token" prefix format. The Bearer format is used for OAuth access tokens, which is different from the token generated by the GitHub App in the workflow.
```suggestion
    "Authorization": f"token {GH_TOKEN}",
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
When GH_TOKEN is empty (not set in the environment), the Authorization header will still be included with an empty Bearer token. This could cause authentication errors. Consider conditionally including the Authorization header only when GH_TOKEN is not empty, or including both authenticated and unauthenticated request paths.
```suggestion
    "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
}
if GH_TOKEN:
    HEADERS["Authorization"] = f"Bearer {GH_TOKEN}"
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: **Accepted (with a different fix than suggested).** Good catch — it's actually worse than described: a malformed pattern in `[[ ... == ... ]]` raises a *fatal* zsh error that aborts the entire calling scope, even with stderr redirected. The suggested `setopt no_bad_pattern` provably does **not** help here — that option only affects filename globbing, not conditional pattern matching (verified empirically on zsh 5.9). Fixed in 2ec23617 by containing the error with zsh's `{ ... } always { (( TRY_BLOCK_ERROR )) && TRY_BLOCK_ERROR=0 }` mechanism: a malformed entry is treated as non-matching and all subsequent list entries are still evaluated.
author: thomaswitt

body: **Accepted.** Fixed in 2ec23617: each line now strips a trailing `\r` (CRLF tolerance) and leading/trailing whitespace before the blank/comment check, so whitespace-only lines and indented `#` comments are ignored. One deliberate trade-off: trimming trailing whitespace means a (pathological) directory name ending in a space won't round-trip through the always/never prompt — accidental trailing whitespace causing silent mismatches is the far more common failure mode, so trimming wins. Behavior is documented in the README.

body: `always { ... }` is not valid following a plain `{ ... }` block in zsh grammar; it’s only valid as part of a `try { ... } always { ... }` construct. As written, this will cause a parse error when the plugin is sourced. Use a proper `try ... always` block (or remove `always` entirely and handle the non-fatal pattern error via options/traps), but ensure the file remains syntactically valid in all supported zsh versions.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `${~line}` forces filename generation on the pattern, which can expand entries like `*` into a large list of filesystem paths (and potentially multiple words), changing the semantics and potentially making the `[[ ... ]]` test invalid or slow. For pattern matching in `[[ $a == pattern ]]`, avoid forced filename generation; use the variable as the pattern directly, and (if you want extended operators like `(...)`, `^`, etc.) enable `EXTENDED_GLOB` locally instead of glob-expanding the pattern.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The docs link to “Glob Operators”, which includes extended-glob syntax that requires `EXTENDED_GLOB` to be enabled. The implementation currently doesn’t show that option being enabled locally in the matcher, so patterns using extended operators may not work as implied. Either document that only basic wildcard patterns are supported by default, or update the matcher to enable `EXTENDED_GLOB` locally so the documentation matches actual behavior.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: **Rejected — this is not zsh grammar.** zsh has no `try` keyword at all; per zshmisc *Complex Commands* the documented syntax is exactly `{ try-list } always { always-list }`, i.e. an `always` block follows a plain `{ ... }` block. The construct has existed since zsh 4.2 (well below Oh My Zsh's minimum supported version). Verified: the file passes `zsh -n` and the function executes correctly under zsh 5.9 — there is no parse error. (Independently confirmed by a second review pass with OpenAI Codex.)
author: thomaswitt

body: **Rejected — this describes bash semantics, not zsh.** (1) Filename generation is never performed on the operands of `[[ ... ]]` conditional expressions in zsh; `${~line}` merely enables GLOB_SUBST so the value is *interpreted as a pattern* in the pattern-matching context. Verified empirically: with `line='*'` in a directory full of files, `[[ /some/path == ${~line} ]]` matches the string — nothing expands to a file list. (2) The suggested fix would break the entire feature: unlike bash, zsh matches an unquoted RHS variable in `[[ == ]]` **literally**. `line='/a/*'; [[ /a/b == $line ]]` is false, `[[ /a/b == ${~line} ]]` is true — `${~}` is precisely what makes glob entries work. (Independently confirmed by a second review pass with OpenAI Codex.)
author: thomaswitt

body: **Accepted (docs-only fix).** Correct: under `emulate -L zsh` EXTENDED_GLOB is off, so `#`, `^` and `~` do not act as pattern operators (verified), while `*`, `?`, `[abc]` and `(a|b)` alternation work. Fixed in 8d5b8c21 by removing the Glob-Operators link and stating explicitly that only the basic operators are supported and EXTENDED_GLOB operators are **not** enabled. Deliberately *not* enabling EXTENDED_GLOB in the matcher: pre-existing or hand-written list entries containing literal `#`/`^`/`~` (e.g. `/srv/www/site~staging`) match literally today and would silently change allow/deny semantics — a bad compatibility break for a security-relevant list. (Prompt-written entries would be safe either way, since `${(b)...}` escapes extended-glob metacharacters too.)

Hi Thomas, thanks for the contribution! Before we merge this, I think we'd need to see some more work on improving the UX. 

For starters, the `.list` files are autogenerated by our functions, we'd have to improve the usability of such files more than "edit a file in path X and add a glob". Currently it requires quite a lot of expertise to configure. 

Ideally we'd surface some commands for editing, but the first step is to improve the docs to clarify where they are. 

Also, I'd like to avoid accidentally allowing more than the user intends. A step in that direction is making the globbing more specific (using filename globbing) instead of string matching.

`HOME` fallback uses `eval echo ~$USER` and the `getent passwd $USER` call leaves `$USER` unquoted. If a caller has a crafted `USER` in the environment, this can result in unintended command execution via `eval` (and word-splitting in `getent`). body:
The trailing-slash normalization for `OMZ_DIR` appears incorrect: `_omz_safe=${OMZ_DIR%"${OMZ_DIR##*[!/]"}}` will collapse to an empty string when `OMZ_DIR` ends with `/` (because the `##*[!/]` pattern won’t match), causing the safety check to treat otherwise valid paths like `/home/user/.oh-my-zsh/` as unsafe and abort the uninstall. Consider normalizing with a POSIX-safe loop that strips `/` until none remain (or equivalent), then run the safety case check against the normalized value.
```suggestion
_omz_safe=$OMZ_DIR
while [ "$_omz_safe" != "/" ] && [ "${_omz_safe%/}" != "$_omz_safe" ]; do
  _omz_safe=${_omz_safe%/}
done
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Verified in `/bin/sh` — the normalization handles all cases correctly:

| Input | `_omz_safe` | Result |
|---|---|---|
| `/home/user/.oh-my-zsh` | `/home/user/.oh-my-zsh` | Allowed ✓ |
| `/home/user/.oh-my-zsh/` | `/home/user/.oh-my-zsh` | Allowed ✓ |
| `/home/user/.oh-my-zsh///` | `/home/user/.oh-my-zsh` | Allowed ✓ |
| `///` | `/` (fallback) | Blocked ✓ |
| (empty) | `/` (fallback) | Blocked ✓ |

`${var##*[!/]}` removes the longest prefix ending with a non-slash character, isolating only the trailing slashes. `${var%...}` then strips them. The pattern *does* match whenever the string contains at least one non-slash character — which any valid OMZ path will.

The `${_omz_safe:-/}` fallback handles the degenerate case where all characters are slashes (empty result → falls back to `/` → caught by the case guard).

body: ## Pull request overview Adds a `-t/--to-directory ` option to the `extract` plugin so users can extract archives into a specified directory instead of the current one, addressing issue #13090. The change rewrites argument parsing as a loop to support multiple flags, prefixes the per-archive `extract_dir` with the target directory, and advertises the new flag in the `_extract` completion. **Changes:** - Replace the single `-r` check with a loop-based parser handling `-r/--remove` and `-t/--to-directory ` (with validation that the directory exists). - Prefix `extract_dir` with `target_directory` so archives are unpacked under the requested path. - Add a `-t/--to-directory` entry to the zsh completion. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | plugins/extract/extract.plugin.zsh | Parse new flag, validate target dir, and route extraction into it | | plugins/extract/_extract | Expose `-t/--to-directory` in shell completion | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
When `-t/--to-directory` is used, `extract_dir` becomes a path like `target/name` rather than a bare basename, which breaks the post-extraction flattening logic below (lines 144–174):

- Line 160: `"${content[1]:t}" == "$extract_dir"` compares a basename to a path, so the "extracted folder has the same name as `extract_dir`" branch never triggers, and `rmdir "$extract_dir"` would also fail because `cd "$pwd"` already happened.
- Lines 168–170: `mv "${content[1]}" .` moves the single extracted item into the original `$pwd` instead of into `$target_directory`, defeating the purpose of `-t`. The subsequent `rmdir "$extract_dir"` also runs from `$pwd`, not from inside the target directory.

The flattening logic needs to operate relative to `$target_directory` (e.g. compare against `${extract_dir:t}`, and `mv` into `"${target_directory:-.}"`) when a target directory is provided, otherwise extracted files end up in the wrong place.

body:
## Pull request overview This PR replaces the dotenv plugin's previous `source`-based loading with a custom safe parser. The new parser tokenizes the file with zsh's `(Z:C:)` flag, rejects entries that contain command substitution outside of safe (single-quote) contexts, blocks assignments to a curated list of security-sensitive variables and zsh specials, enforces a 10 MiB size cap, handles FIFOs via `sysread` with the same cap, and flips the interactive prompt default from "yes" to "no". A zunit test suite (basic parsing, security, dotenv-js compatibility) and a `tests/_support/bootstrap` harness are introduced alongside README updates. **Changes:** - Introduces `_parse_dotenv_content` / `parse_dotenv` as a hardened replacement for `source`, with forbidden-variable filtering, command-substitution detection, escaped-`$` handling and in-file variable expansion. - Adds size-limited FIFO reading (`_dotenv_read_limited`), pre-source syntax validation (`_dotenv_check_syntax`), and changes the confirmation prompt default to "no". - Adds a zunit test suite under `plugins/dotenv/tests/` (basic-parsing, security, compatibility, fixtures, bootstrap helpers) and rewrites the README's Security/Tests sections. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 10 out of 10 changed files in this pull request and generated 5 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | plugins/dotenv/dotenv.plugin.zsh | Adds the new parser, FIFO reader, syntax checker, and changes the prompt default. | | plugins/dotenv/README.md | Documents multi-line/in-file expansion, tests, and the new security guarantees; removes the old disclaimer. | | plugins/dotenv/.zunit.yml | zunit configuration for the new test suite. | | plugins/dotenv/tests/_support/bootstrap | Test bootstrap with parse helpers, allexport sourcing helper, and custom zunit assertions. | | plugins/dotenv/tests/_support/fixtures/dotenvjs.env | Fixture mirroring the dotenv-js test corpus for parity checks. | | plugins/dotenv/tests/_support/fixtures/features.env | Fixture exercising export/expansion/multiline edge cases. | | plugins/dotenv/tests/basic-parsing.zunit | Core parser behavior and FIFO/size-limit tests. | | plugins/dotenv/tests/security.zunit | Command-substitution and forbidden-variable filter tests. | | plugins/dotenv/tests/compatibility.zunit | Compares parser output against `source`+`allexport` for fixtures. | | plugins/dotenv/tests/_output/.gitignore | Ignores zunit's per-run output directory. |

body:
The intent here (per the comment block above) is to concatenate tokens belonging to the same logical command until a `;` separator is encountered. However, line 58 unconditionally overwrites `line` with the current `node`, which discards both the previously-accumulated content and the space appended on line 57. As a result, only the last token before each `;` is preserved.

For example, with `export FOO=bar`, the tokens produced by `(Z:C:)` are `export`, `FOO=bar`, `;`, but `line` ends up equal to `FOO=bar` rather than `export FOO=bar`. The `export ` strip on line 91 then becomes a no-op (the regex still matches by coincidence). More importantly, multi-token logical commands cannot be reassembled correctly, which limits the parser to cases where the final token happens to be `KEY=VALUE`.

Line 58 should append (`line+="$node"`) rather than assign.

body:
This test does not populate `expected_vars`, so the assertion only succeeds when `DOTENV_TEST_VARS` is empty (i.e., when every assignment in the fixture was filtered out). That correctly validates the forbidden-variable filter, but it also means the test would silently pass if `parse_dotenv` ever errored out before producing any output (or simply did nothing). Consider asserting a non-empty positive case alongside it — e.g., include a benign `ALLOWED=ok` assignment in the fixture and put it in `expected_vars` — so the test cannot pass for the wrong reason.

Also note that the surrounding `@setup` removes the temporary `fixture` file via `command rm -f "$fixture"` in `@teardown`, but this test instead uses `=(<<EOF ... EOF)` which creates its own ephemeral file. That works, but it diverges from the file-fixture pattern used by every other test in this file without explanation.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This is a user-facing behavior change: previously the prompt defaulted to "yes" (pressing Enter or any other key sourced the file), now any non-explicit key — including Enter — declines. While this is a sensible security default and is listed in the PR description, existing users with muscle memory will experience their `.env` files silently no longer being loaded after upgrading. Consider calling this change out prominently in the README's Settings/Security section (and/or in release notes) so users aren't surprised.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `_parse_dotenv_content` does not reset `DOTENV_TEST_VARS` before populating it. Because it's declared with `typeset -gA` (global), entries from a previous call persist and are merged with the new results. The `unset DOTENV_TEST_VARS` in `@teardown` masks this in tests, but any caller that invokes `parse_dotenv ... test` more than once in the same shell (or with the array pre-existing) will see stale keys. Consider clearing the array before the loop (e.g., `DOTENV_TEST_VARS=()`) or assigning it from a local array at the very end.

body: ## Pull request overview This PR updates the `per-directory-history` plugin to avoid calling `fc -p` from the `zshaddhistory` hook (which was breaking preexec argument passing), and instead performs history-context switching during initialization / mode changes. **Changes:** - Removed `fc -p` from `_per-directory-history-addhistory` to prevent corruption of `preexec`’s `$1`. - Added `fc -p` calls to `_per-directory-history-set-directory-history` and `_per-directory-history-set-global-history` to make the context switch happen outside per-command hooks. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: `fc -p` is currently guarded by `[[ -e ... ]]`, so when the per-directory history file doesn’t exist yet (common for a new directory), the history context never gets switched. That likely prevents per-directory history from persisting when you enter a new dir and exit without `cd` (since zsh will keep using the previous history context). Consider calling `fc -p` unconditionally and only guarding the `fc -R` load.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Same issue as above: `fc -p` is only run when `$HISTFILE` already exists. If the user points `HISTFILE` at a new path (or the file is missing for any reason), the global-history context won’t be switched, leaving the plugin in an inconsistent state. Call `fc -p` unconditionally and only guard the `fc -R` read.
